home.title: OpenEPI
home.subtitle: Epidemiology and Biostatistics Tools
home.searchDescription: Press Cmd+K to open the command palette
home.twoByTwoDesc: Contingency analysis, odds ratio and relative risk.

settings.title: Settings
settings.customizeApp: Customize the application according to your preferences
settings.accessibilityDescription: Visual comfort and reduction of animations
settings.reduceAnimationsDescription: Disables or simplifies transitions and animations
settings.highContrastDescription: Increases color contrast for better readability
settings.compactModeDescription: Reduces margins and paddings to display more content
settings.enableNotificationsDescription: Display toast notifications in the application
settings.notificationDurationDescription: Time before automatic closure of the notification
settings.soundNotificationsDescription: Play a sound when a notification appears
settings.hapticFeedbackDescription: Make the device vibrate during notifications (if supported)
settings.settingsSaved: Settings are automatically saved in your browser.

stdMortalityCalculator.title: SMR Calculator
stdMortalityCalculator.subtitle: Mortality statistics and epidemiology.
stdMortalityCalculator.footnote: * For small numbers (observed ≤ 5), prefer exact methods (Fisher, Mid‑P).
stdMortalityCalculator.jstatMissing: jStat library not detected – exact intervals and p‑values are replaced by approximations. For accurate calculations, include jStat in your project.
stdMortalityCalculator.interpretationNotSignif: The difference is not statistically significant (CI contains 1.0).
stdMortalityCalculator.reportFooter: SMR Calculator – Statistical tool for epidemiology
stdMortalityCalculator.helpPrinciple: The SMR compares observed deaths in a specific population to the expected deaths if that population had the same mortality rates as the general population.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpCi: The CI indicates the reliability of the result. If it contains the value 1, the result is not statistically significant.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpExactDesc: Based on Poisson distribution and χ² law. Reference for small numbers.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpMidPDesc: Continuity correction of the exact test, less conservative.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpByarDesc: Rothman & Boice (1979) approximation, very accurate even for O > 5.

introduction.subtitle: Comprehensive guide to biostatistics, simulations, geospatial analysis, workspace, and explorer modules.

stdMortalityCalculator.title: SMR Calculator
stdMortalityCalculator.subtitle: Epidemiology and mortality statistics.
stdMortalityCalculator.footnote: * For small counts (observed ≤ 5), prefer exact methods (Fisher, Mid‑P).
stdMortalityCalculator.interpretationSignifHigh: The excess is statistically significant (CI entirely > 1.0).
stdMortalityCalculator.interpretationSignifLow: The reduction is statistically significant (CI entirely < 1.0).
stdMortalityCalculator.interpretationNotSignif: The difference is not statistically significant (CI contains 1.0).
stdMortalityCalculator.helpPrinciple: The Standardized Mortality Ratio (SMR) is the ratio of observed deaths in the study population to the deaths that would be expected if this population had the same mortality rates as the reference population.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpRiskExcess: Excess mortality compared to the reference.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpCi: A CI that does not include 1.0 indicates a statistically significant difference at the chosen level.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpExactDesc: Garwood (1936) exact CI based on χ² distribution. Exact two-sided Poisson test. Reference method used by OpenEpi.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpMidPDesc: Lancaster (1961) Mid-P test. Less conservative than the exact test. CI computed by numerical bisection.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpByarDesc: Byar cube-root approximation (Rothman & Boice, 1979). Reliable for obs ≥ 1. p-value by CI inversion.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpRgDesc: Log-normal CI: exp(ln(SMR) ± z/√obs). Rothman & Greenland (1998). Not defined for obs = 0.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpChi2Desc: Pearson χ² test (1 df). No CI provided (consistent with OpenEpi).
stdMortalityCalculator.reportFooter: Auto-generated report — for epidemiological purposes only.

twoByTwoTable.title: 2×2 Tables
twoByTwoTable.description: Evaluation of the association between exposure and disease.
twoByTwoTable.explanation: 2×2 tables cross exposure (yes/no) with disease (yes/no). Produced statistics include Fisher's exact and mid-p tests, chi-squares, odds ratio, relative risk, risk difference, and etiological fractions.

proportions.title: Proportions
proportions.description: Estimation of a proportion and its confidence interval.
proportions.exactClopperPearson: • Exact (Clopper-Pearson): Conservative, based on binomial distribution
proportions.midPExact: • Mid-P exact: Less conservative than Clopper-Pearson
proportions.useCaseDescription: Out of 500 people tested, 75 are positive. What is the true prevalence?

proportionsSample.title: Sample Size for a Proportion
proportionsSample.description: Calculates the required sample size to estimate a proportion.
proportionsSample.explanation: This module determines the number of subjects needed to estimate a proportion with given precision.
proportionsSample.exampleDescription: To estimate a prevalence of 20% with ±3% precision and 95% confidence:

oneRate.title: One Rate Test
oneRate.description: Inference on an incidence or mortality rate.
oneRate.explanation: Compares an observed rate to a reference rate or calculates its confidence interval. Uses Poisson distribution for rare events.
oneRate.exampleDescription: In a cohort of 10,000 people followed for 5 years, 25 cases are observed.

compareTwoRates.title: Comparison of Two Rates
compareTwoRates.description: Test for equality of two incidence rates.
compareTwoRates.explanation: Compares two rates based on person-time data. Calculates the rate ratio and rate difference with their confidence intervals, as well as the Mantel-Haenszel test.

anova.title: One-Way ANOVA
anova.description: Comparison of means for three or more groups.
anova.explanation: ANOVA (Analysis of Variance) tests the null hypothesis that the means of several groups are equal. It decomposes total variance into between-group and within-group variance.
anova.anovaTable: ANOVA Table: Sum of squares, degrees of freedom, mean squares

tTest.title: Student's t-Test
tTest.explanation: The t-test compares the means of two independent groups. Two versions are provided: Student's t-test (equal variances) and Welch's t-test (unequal variances).
tTest.hartleyFTest: Hartley's F Test to compare variances

meanConfidenceInterval.title: Confidence Interval for a Mean
meanConfidenceInterval.description: Estimation of a population mean from a sample.
meanConfidenceInterval.explanation: Calculates the confidence interval for a mean from sample descriptive statistics (n, mean, standard deviation). Uses Student's t distribution.

meanDifferencePower.title: Power for a Mean Difference
meanDifferencePower.description: Calculates the power of a mean comparison test.
meanDifferencePower.explanation: Power is the probability of rejecting the null hypothesis when it is false. This module calculates it from sample sizes, expected difference, and variability.

meanDifferenceSample.title: Sample Size for Mean Difference
meanDifferenceSample.description: Determines the number of subjects needed.
meanDifferenceSample.explanation: Calculates the required sample size to detect a given difference between two means, with specified power and confidence level.

medianPercentileCi.title: Confidence Interval for Median and Percentiles
medianPercentileCi.explanation: Calculates confidence intervals for the median and percentiles (25th, 75th, etc.) using binomial or rank-based methods.

unmatchedCaseControl.title: Unmatched Case-Control Analysis
unmatchedCaseControl.description: Analysis of exposure-disease association in case-control studies.
unmatchedCaseControl.explanation: This module analyzes data from an unmatched case-control study. It calculates the odds ratio (OR), its 95% confidence interval, and performs the Mantel-Haenszel χ² test to test the association.

sampleSizeUnmatchedCaseControl.title: Sample Size (Unmatched Case-Control)
sampleSizeUnmatchedCaseControl.description: Calculates the number of cases and controls needed.
sampleSizeUnmatchedCaseControl.explanation: This module calculates the sample size for an unmatched case-control study. Users input the desired confidence level, power, proportion of exposure in controls, and either an odds ratio or the proportion of exposure in cases.

matchedCaseControl.title: Matched Case-Control Analysis
matchedCaseControl.explanation: For matched case-control studies, this module calculates the Mantel-Haenszel odds ratio, confidence interval, and McNemar's test. It handles 1:1, 1:n, and M:N matchings.

cohort.title: Cohort Study Analysis
cohort.description: Calculation of relative risk, risk difference, and attributable fractions.
cohort.explanation: In cohort studies, exposed and non-exposed groups are followed over time. This module calculates the relative risk (RR), risk difference (RD), and etiological fractions in exposed and in the population.

rct.title: Randomized Controlled Trial (RCT)
rct.description: Specific analysis for randomized clinical trials.
rct.explanation: Module dedicated to randomized trials. Calculates relative risk, absolute risk reduction (ARR), number needed to treat (NNT), and corresponding confidence intervals.

screening.title: Diagnostic Test Evaluation
screening.description: Sensitivity, specificity, predictive values, likelihood ratios.
screening.explanation: This module evaluates the performance of a diagnostic test against a gold standard. It calculates sensitivity, specificity, positive and negative predictive values, likelihood ratios, Youden's index, and Cohen's Kappa coefficient.

doseResponse.title: Dose-Response (Chi-Square Trend)
doseResponse.description: Linear trend test for more than two exposure levels.
doseResponse.explanation: This module performs the Mantel-Haenszel linear trend chi-square test for data with more than two exposure levels (e.g., increasing doses). It calculates the 1 degree of freedom p-value to test the hypothesis of a linear relationship between exposure and risk.
doseResponse.multipleStrata: Multiple strata can be entered to control for confounding factors.

rByCTable.title: R x C Table
rByCTable.description: Analysis of contingency tables of any size.
rByCTable.explanation: This module generalizes the analysis of 2×2 tables to tables of dimension R (rows) × C (columns). It calculates Pearson's χ², likelihood ratio (G²), contingency coefficient, and Cramer's V. It can also analyze multiple strata.
rByCTable.associationBloodGroupDisease: Association between blood group and disease (3 groups × 4 phenotypes)

randomNumbers.title: Random Number Generator
randomNumbers.description: Generation of random numbers for randomization and simulations.
randomNumbers.explanation: This module generates random numbers according to different distributions: uniform, normal, binomial, Poisson. It can also generate sequences for clinical trial randomization (permuted blocks).

simulation.description: Simulate the spread of an infectious disease between interconnected regions, with compartmental models and interventions.
simulation.explanation1: This module allows simulating the evolution of an epidemic on a network of regions (e.g., countries, departments). Each region has its own population and compartments (S, E, I, R, D, Q depending on the chosen model). Regions are connected by links modeling population flows (mobility), influencing inter-regional dynamics.
simulation.explanation2: Calculations use ordinary differential equations (ODE) in discrete time. The user can adjust epidemiological parameters, add interventions (lockdown, vaccination) and observe the real-time impact on an interactive map, graphs, or dynamic network.
simulation.sir: SIR: S, I, R - Base model without incubation period
simulation.seir: SEIR: S, E, I, R - Adds an exposure phase (E)
simulation.seird: SEIRD: S, E, I, R, D - Includes mortality (D)
simulation.seiqrd: SEIQRD: S, E, I, Q, R, D - Adds quarantine (Q)
simulation.interactiveMap: Interactive Map: 2D (Leaflet) or 3D (Globe) with markers proportional to population and mobility flows.
simulation.evolutionCurves: Evolution Curves: Tracking of compartments S, E, I, R, D over time. Ability to compare two simulations.
simulation.dynamicGraph: Dynamic Graph: Force-directed visualization, with nodes colored by infection rate, links weighted by mobility.
simulation.detailedData: Detailed Data: Complete list of regions with their compartment counts and infection rates.
simulation.massVaccination: Mass Vaccination: β reduced, 40% efficacy at D30
simulation.lateIntervention: Late Intervention: lockdown at D50, R₀=3.0
simulation.map: Map: Type: 2D (Leaflet) / 3D (Globe) | Theme: light, dark, satellite | Marker Shape: circle / icon | Line Opacity (mobility)
simulation.graphs: Graphs: Line Style: continuous / dotted | Adjustable Thickness | Fill Opacity under curves | Displayable/hidable Grid | Annotations for interventions
simulation.network: Network: Node Shape: circle, square, triangle | Link Style: continuous / dotted | Repulsion Force (charge) | Clustering by region (based on cluster attribute)
simulation.step1: Select a model (SEIRD by default) and a scenario (Base).
simulation.step2: Click Start to launch the simulation. Observe the evolution on the map and graphs.
simulation.step3: Add an intervention (lockdown) via the dedicated button to see its impact on the infected curve.
simulation.step4: Switch to comparison mode to simulate an alternative scenario with different parameters and compare the curves.
simulation.keyboardShortcuts: Keyboard Shortcuts (3D map mode): arrows to rotate the globe, R to recenter, 2/3 to change projection.
simulation.technicalNote: Note: The simulation uses a discrete time step of 1 day. Equations are based on standard compartmental models. Inter-region mobility is modeled by a diffusion term proportional to the prevalence difference.

geospatial.description: Based on Leaflet and react-leaflet – explore your epidemiological data on dynamic maps.
geospatial.explanation1: The geospatial module allows loading, visualizing, and analyzing georeferenced data (points, incidents, incidence rates…). It offers three complementary representation modes: individual markers, clustering for large volumes, and heatmap for visualizing densities.
geospatial.explanation2: Data can be imported from CSV or Excel files, or via integrated examples (historical epidemics, continental data). An integrated AI analysis automatically provides summaries, trends, and alerts.
geospatial.dataImport: Data Import: Drag-and-drop or select your files. Automatic detection of latitude/longitude and intensity value columns.
geospatial.multiLayers: Multi-Layers: Each dataset is an independent layer. Show/hide, change color, radius, or opacity of each series.
geospatial.integratedAIAnalysis: Integrated AI Analysis: Automatically generate a summary, trends, recommendations, and risk alerts from visible data.
geospatial.individualPoints: Individual Points: Each data is represented by a circle whose size and color vary according to intensity (e.g., number of cases). Ideal for small datasets.
geospatial.pointsParameters: Parameters: global radius, opacity, color per dataset
geospatial.clustering: Clustering: Automatically groups nearby points into clusters whose size indicates the number of elements. Recommended for more than 500 points.
geospatial.clusteringOptions: Options: cluster color, text color, max cluster radius, disable threshold at zoom
geospatial.heatmap: Heatmap: Visualizes point density weighted by intensity. Ideal for identifying clusters or risk areas.
geospatial.heatmapSettings: Settings: radius, blur, color gradient, minimum opacity
geospatial.heatmapConfig: Heatmap: Radius – heat point size (default 25) | Blur – gradient softening (default 15) | Minimum Opacity – transparency of cold areas | Customizable Gradient – e.g., 0.4 → blue, 1.0 → red
geospatial.clusteringConfig: Clustering: Cluster color and text color | Max cluster radius (grouping distance) | Disable threshold – at which zoom clusters display as individual points | Spiderfy – spider layout at maximum zoom
geospatial.note: Note: Each dataset retains its own visibility, clustering, heatmap, color, radius, and opacity settings. Global parameters serve as default values.
geospatial.summary: Summary: Description of clusters, correlations, global trends
geospatial.insights: Insights: Key facts: risk zone, increase, under-reporting
geospatial.alerts: Alerts: Risk level (low / moderate / high) and attention points
geospatial.step1: Load a CSV file containing latitude, longitude, and cases columns.
geospatial.step2: Enable clustering to visualize global distribution, then switch to heatmap to identify hotspots.
geospatial.step3: In the configuration panel, adjust the dataset color, point radius, and opacity.
geospatial.step4: Click Analyze to get an AI report with risk level and recommendations.

workspace.description: Run R code directly in your browser.
workspace.explanation1: The workspace embeds a complete R engine based on WebR (R port to WebAssembly). You can create, open, and save multiple .R scripts, run the entire code or just one line, and visualize results immediately in the console or as graphs.
workspace.explanation2: The following features are available: script execution, file management, interactive console, graph capture, image download, installation of common packages (ggplot2, dplyr, …). Some parts (advanced animation, full support for all packages) are still under improvement and marked as beta.
workspace.multiFileEditor: Multi-File Editor: Tabs to manage multiple scripts simultaneously. Rename, duplicate, delete via context menu. Automatic saving in the browser (localStorage).
workspace.interactiveConsoleFeature: Interactive Console: Enter R commands live, navigate history with arrows, view text outputs and graphs in the same area.
workspace.graphicalVisualizations: Graphical Visualizations: All graphs generated by R are automatically captured and displayed. Grid mode, animation mode, individual download, and full-screen enlargement.
workspace.packageManagement: Package Management: Install packages from the webR repository (e.g., ggplot2, dplyr, KernSmooth) via the dedicated field in the header or directly with webr::install().
workspace.animationBetaDetail: Animation (beta): If your script produces multiple graphs (e.g., in a loop), you can view them in grid mode or start an animation. The button allows playing/pausing the animation. Manual navigation is also possible.
workspace.animationNote: The animation works for pre-generated sequences, but continuous playback of dynamic graphs is still under optimization.
workspace.packageInstallationDetail: Package Installation: WebR allows installing pre-compiled packages for WebAssembly. In the header, a dedicated field lets you quickly install a package (e.g., ggplot2). Installation logs appear in the console.
workspace.packageNote: Packages requiring system dependencies or native compilation may not be available.
workspace.codeExample:
# Mobile epidemic curve
x <- seq(-5, 10, length.out = 200)
for (i in 1:40) {
  mu <- i / 8
  y <- dnorm(x, mean = mu, sd = 1.5)
  plot(x, y, type="n", ylim=c(0, 0.3), axes=FALSE)
  polygon(c(x, rev(x)), c(y, rep(0, 200)), col="#3b82f622", border=NA)
  lines(x, y, col="#3b82f6", lwd=3)
  Sys.sleep(0.04)
}
workspace.step3: Click the Launch button to run the entire script. You will see an animation appear in the console.
workspace.step4: Use the navigation arrows or the button to control the animation. Export an image by clicking the icon.

explorer.description: Access over 35 million biomedical references directly from your workspace.
explorer.explanation1: The explorer integrates a complete interface to the PubMed database (NCBI). You can search by keywords, filter by year and publication type, sort by relevance or date, and view detailed abstracts.
explorer.explanation2: A unique MeSH query generation feature uses PubMed's automatic translation to enrich your search terms with appropriate MeSH descriptors. Articles can be saved locally (favorites) and exported in RIS format for your bibliographic management software.
explorer.advancedSearch: Advanced Search: Keywords, filters by period (e.g., 2010-2025), article types (review, clinical trial, meta-analysis…), sort by relevance or publication date.
explorer.meshGenerator: MeSH Generator: From a free expression, retrieve the MeSH translation proposed by PubMed, including controlled terms and field tags (e.g., "[PTYP]").
explorer.favoritesExport: Favorites & Export: Save locally up to 200 articles. Export a results page or all favorites in RIS format (compatible with EndNote, Zotero, Mendeley).
explorer.home: Home: Search bar with filter | Direct access to favorites from the bottom link | Display of MeSH suggestions in a separate panel
explorer.results: Results: Quick toggle between search and favorites | Side panel for article details (abstract, authors, DOI, PMID) | Temporal distribution graph of results
explorer.step1: In centered mode, type covid-19 vaccine.
explorer.step2: Click Generate MeSH to get an optimized query (e.g., "COVID-19 Vaccines"[Mesh]). Apply it.
explorer.step4: Browse results, click an article to see details. Add to favorites (bookmark icon).
explorer.step5: Switch to the Favorites tab, then Export all to generate an RIS file.
